answer:
Yes—there is relevant research, though almost no study is exactly “AI researcher chooses between more interesting research job vs slightly higher-paid less interesting management job.” The closest literatures are work design, person–job fit, intrinsic motivation, pay/job satisfaction, promotion, and regret.

## Short version

Across the evidence, **intrinsically rewarding work—autonomy, skill use, intellectual challenge, meaning, and fit with one’s interests—is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement**. **Actual pay level is usually a much weaker predictor**, especially once basic needs/security are met. However, **leading a team is not merely an extrinsic perk**; if leadership is something you genuinely want for autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth, then it may itself be an intrinsic motivator.

So the research would not say simply “stay with the more interesting work.” It would say:

> Do not trade away day-to-day intrinsic work quality for a modest salary increase/status bump unless the new role gives you other intrinsic goods you strongly value—e.g. leadership, autonomy, impact, growth, or mission.

## 1. Interesting/autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback.

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976**
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design studies

The meta-analytic result is broadly: jobs high in **autonomy, complexity, skill variety, task significance, and feedback** are substantially associated with higher job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. These are not tiny effects; work-design variables often explain a large share of variance in job satisfaction.

For your case, “interesting research” likely bundles several of these: intellectual challenge, autonomy, skill use, meaningfulness, and feedback from reality/experiments. That bundle is exactly the kind of thing the literature says matters a lot.

## 2. Fit with one’s interests and values matters a lot

Another relevant area is **person–job fit** and **vocational interest fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis: person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**, meta-analysis: vocational interest fit predicts satisfaction, performance, persistence, though effect sizes vary.

A rough takeaway: people tend to do better and feel better when the work matches what they are actually interested in and good at. The evidence is not perfect causally—happy people may also perceive more fit—but the association is robust.

For you, the key question is whether Meta is “less interesting” only in topic, or whether it is less fitting in the deeper sense: less aligned with your curiosity, identity, mission, and preferred mode of contribution.

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There is also a literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis: meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.
- **Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski, 2010**, review of meaning in work.

Meaning is especially important for people in mission-driven or research-driven careers. If Anthropic feels meaningful to you in a way Meta does not, that is not a vague “soft” preference; it is a known predictor of satisfaction.

Caveat: meaningful work can also have a dark side—people with a “calling” may accept overwork, under-negotiate, or tolerate bad conditions. So meaning is important, but not infinitely overriding.

## 4. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases are often weaker than people expect

The best-cited job-level result:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: actual pay level has only a weak relationship with job satisfaction, around the low \( r \approx .15 \) range. Pay satisfaction matters more, but still does not dominate intrinsic work factors.

At the broader income/well-being level:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income but showed flattening at higher levels in their data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**: found experienced well-being continues to rise with log income, without a sharp plateau.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023**: reconciliation: for many people well-being keeps rising with income, but marginal gains are smaller and heterogeneous; for the least happy group, high income helps less.

The robust conclusion is not “money doesn’t matter.” It is:

> Money matters a lot when it changes security, freedom, family options, debt, geography, or perceived fairness. But a modest raise at already-high income levels is usually not as large a driver of daily happiness as work content, autonomy, relationships, and meaning.

So if Meta’s salary increase materially changes your life—housing, runway, family, financial independence—that matters. If it is merely “nice to have,” the research would predict a relatively small happiness effect.

## 5. Promotions and leadership have mixed effects

Being promoted or moving into management can increase satisfaction through status, influence, autonomy, and pay. But it can also increase stress, meetings, politics, responsibility, and reduce time spent on the work that originally motivated you.

Relevant work includes:

- **Kosteas, 2011**: promotions and expected promotions are associated with job satisfaction.
- **Boyce & Oswald, 2012**: promotion did not clearly improve overall health and was associated with increased mental strain in some analyses.
- Broader organizational research: autonomy and influence are good; role conflict, overload, and reduced task enjoyment are bad.

For your situation, “leading a small team” is the ambiguous factor. It could be either:

1. **Intrinsic/growth-positive**: mentoring, shaping direction, multiplying impact, learning management, owning a problem area.
2. **Extrinsic/status-positive but daily-negative**: more meetings, less research, less intellectual novelty, more coordination overhead.

The research would treat those very differently. If leadership is a deep self-concordant goal, not just a title, it may be worth a lot.

## 6. Regret: direct evidence is thinner, but there are useful findings

There is less direct evidence on “regretting choosing the interesting job over the higher-paid job.” But regret research gives some clues.

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: education and career are among the most common domains of major life regret.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: people tend to regret actions more in the short run, but inactions more in the long run.
- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**: people often regret failures to pursue their “ideal self” more than failures of duty or obligation.

Applied cautiously: if leading a team is part of your “ideal self,” you may later regret never trying it. Conversely, if frontier research/AI safety/scientific contribution is central to your ideal self, you may regret stepping away for a modestly better package and title.

So the regret question is not “will I regret giving up salary?” but rather:

> Which path would feel more like failing to pursue something central to who I want to become?

## My research-based read of the tradeoff

If the Meta offer is mainly:

- a bit more money,
- a title/management opportunity,
- but substantially less interesting work,
- less mission/fit,
- less autonomy or less intellectual growth,

then the research would generally predict that **the interesting, well-fitting role is more likely to sustain job satisfaction**.

But if the Meta role gives you:

- genuine leadership practice,
- high autonomy,
- a team/problem you can shape,
- meaningful scale,
- a chance to build a capability you deeply want,
- and not too much loss of intellectual engagement,

then it is not simply “less interesting for more pay.” It may be a different intrinsic-growth path.

A practical research-consistent way to decide: compare the expected ordinary week, not the abstract offer. Estimate:

- hours/week of deep technical or research work,
- hours/week of people management and meetings,
- autonomy over agenda,
- quality of collaborators,
- mission/meaning,
- learning rate,
- stress/overload,
- future optionality,
- whether you would be proud of the work in 2–3 years.

The literature strongly suggests that **recurring day-to-day experience** dominates abstract prestige or marginal compensation more than people often anticipate. But a long-standing desire to lead is also a serious intrinsic motive, not something to dismiss.

answer:
Yes. The short version from the research is:

**Intrinsic job qualities — interesting work, autonomy, learning, meaning, skill use, good fit — are usually among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is typically smaller, especially once pay is already high/adequate.**  
However, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk; it can itself satisfy autonomy, mastery, status, relatedness, and growth needs. So the relevant comparison may not be “interesting work vs salary,” but **which role gives you more intrinsically rewarding day-to-day work over the next few years.**

Some relevant findings:

---

## 1. “Interesting work” / intrinsic job characteristics strongly predict job satisfaction

A large body of work in organizational psychology studies this under labels like **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **work design**, **person-job fit**, and **meaningful work**.

### Job Characteristics Model

Classic work by Hackman & Oldham and later meta-analyses finds that jobs are more satisfying when they provide:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- opportunities to learn and use abilities.

Meta-analytic evidence supports this pretty strongly. For example:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, a major meta-analysis of work design, found that motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, and skill variety were substantially related to job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. Autonomy in particular tends to have one of the strongest associations with satisfaction.

The intuitive takeaway: people are generally happier in roles where they have control, are challenged, use valued skills, and can see why their work matters.

---

## 2. Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors

“Is this work interesting to me?” is partly a question of **fit**: fit between your abilities, values, interests, and the job’s actual demands.

A key meta-analysis:

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** reviewed person-environment fit research. They found that **person-job fit** correlated strongly with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. In many studies, fit is a larger predictor of satisfaction than pay or status.

This matters for your case because “less interesting” may mean lower fit with your scientific/engineering motivations. But if management/mentoring/team-building is something you deeply want, then the Meta role may have high fit in a different dimension.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but the job-satisfaction effect is usually modest

Pay is not irrelevant. It affects security, status, perceived fairness, options, and family constraints. But the direct association between pay level and job satisfaction is often surprisingly small.

A frequently cited meta-analysis:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction,” found that the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction was modest — around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction mattered more than absolute pay, but still did not dominate intrinsic job factors.

Interpretation: going from underpaid to fairly paid can matter a lot. But once someone is already well-compensated, a somewhat higher salary often has a smaller effect on day-to-day happiness than autonomy, interest, colleagues, meaning, manager quality, and workload.

Relatedly, broader happiness/income research — e.g. **Kahneman & Deaton 2010**, **Killingsworth 2021**, and later reconciliation work by **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** — suggests income continues to be associated with well-being, but mostly logarithmically: each additional dollar matters less when income is already high. For a highly paid research engineer, the marginal salary increase may be psychologically real but not huge unless it changes your life constraints, savings trajectory, risk tolerance, or sense of fairness.

---

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they overlap. Research on meaningful work finds large associations with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower turnover intention.

For example, meta-analytic work such as **Allan et al. 2019** finds that meaningful work is strongly related to job satisfaction and work engagement.

This is especially relevant for roles in frontier AI, where people may value the mission, intellectual frontier, or societal relevance. If your current role feels unusually meaningful, that is not a trivial variable; research would suggest it is central to long-run work well-being.

---

## 5. Intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation: intrinsic goals tend to predict well-being better

Self-determination theory — associated with **Deci & Ryan** — distinguishes between motivations satisfying:

- autonomy,
- competence,
- relatedness,

versus more external rewards like money, prestige, or approval.

Across many studies, satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness predicts better well-being and motivation at work. See e.g. **Deci, Olafsen & Ryan 2017** for a review of self-determination theory in organizations.

A relevant nuance: management can be intrinsically motivating if it gives you autonomy, competence growth, mentoring, and impact. But if the team-lead role is mostly meetings, alignment, bureaucracy, and less intellectually alive work, the title/pay may not compensate psychologically.

---

## 6. People often mispredict how much salary/status will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research suggests people often overweight salient changes — money, prestige, title — and underweight repeated daily experiences.

Classic relevant work includes **Kahneman et al.** on focusing illusions and **Gilbert** on affective forecasting. The general result is not “money does not matter,” but rather:

> People often overestimate the long-term happiness impact of visible life changes and underestimate the importance of day-to-day experience.

For jobs, day-to-day experience means: What problems do you solve? With whom? Under what autonomy? With what stress? How much learning? How many annoying meetings? How much alignment with your values?

---

## 7. Regret evidence is less direct, but there are clues

There is less clean research on “do people regret leaving interesting jobs for higher-paying but less interesting ones?” That exact question is hard to study causally.

But regret research gives some hints:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that common life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and family — domains where people perceive lost opportunity.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that in the short term people often regret actions, but in the long term they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.

Applied cautiously: if leading a team is something you have “always wanted to do,” not trying it could become a regret. But if your current Anthropic work is unusually rare, meaningful, and intellectually alive, leaving that frontier may also become a regret. The regret literature does not say “always choose novelty” or “always choose interesting work”; it says people regret foregone personally meaningful opportunities.

---

## 8. Best research-backed framing for your decision

The literature would suggest not asking only:

> “Should I choose interesting work or more salary/team lead?”

Instead ask:

> “Which option better satisfies autonomy, mastery, meaning, fit, learning, and relationships over the next 2–4 years?”

A practical research-aligned checklist:

| Factor | Research implication |
|---|---|
| Interesting daily problems | Strong predictor of satisfaction |
| Autonomy/control | Very strong predictor |
| Learning/growth | Strong predictor, especially for high-skill workers |
| Meaning/mission | Strong predictor of engagement and satisfaction |
| Person-job fit | One of the strongest predictors |
| Manager/team quality | Very important for daily affect and retention |
| Pay increase | Matters, but often modest at high income unless life-changing |
| Leadership opportunity | Could be intrinsically rewarding if you value mentoring/influence |
| Workload/stress/commute | Can dominate if substantially worse |
| Reversibility | Important for regret/risk, though less studied directly |

---

## Bottom line from the evidence

The strongest empirical pattern is:

**People tend to be happier and more engaged when their work is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to them. Pay increases matter, but usually less than people expect once compensation is already high.**

But in your specific case, the Meta offer includes something potentially important: **leading a small team**. If that gives you genuine growth, autonomy, mentoring satisfaction, and career capital, it may count as “interesting work” in a different form. If it is mostly a higher-paying but less meaningful/less intellectually engaging role, research would generally predict a real risk of lower satisfaction over time despite the salary bump.

In contrast, salary seems to have a smaller impact on overall satisfaction. This is intriguing because I wonder how meaningful work and motivation might outweigh financial rewards for many people. **Evaluating promotions and job satisfaction**

I’ll assume the user’s role as a research engineer aligns with high incomes, mentioning companies like Anthropic and Meta. It’s important to avoid assumptions about their financial needs.

**Formulating job satisfaction nuances**

However, there’s strong evidence from job redesign studies showing that enhancing autonomy, task significance, and feedback can lead to better outcomes. 

I want to look for meta-analysis on job redesign, like the work of Parker, Morgeson & Johns (2017) and Knight & Parker (2021) on job crafting interventions, but I shouldn’t overwhelm the user with too much detail.

answer:
Short version: yes, there is quite a lot of research suggesting that **interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being**. Salary matters, but at already-high income levels a “bit higher salary” would usually be expected to have a relatively small marginal effect. However, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk: if management/mentorship is intrinsically important to you, it may satisfy autonomy, competence, relatedness, and growth needs, which are also strongly linked to well-being.

I do **not** know of a clean study exactly like “people who stayed in the more intellectually interesting job vs people who left for a higher-paid less interesting leadership job.” But several adjacent literatures are relevant.

## 1. Job satisfaction is meaningfully related to overall happiness

Job satisfaction is not everything, but it correlates substantially with life satisfaction and mental health.

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and later meta-analytic work found a sizable relationship between **job satisfaction and life satisfaction**, often around the `r ≈ .4` range.
- **Faragher, Cass & Cooper 2005**, a meta-analysis, found job satisfaction associated with lower burnout, depression, anxiety, and physical illness.

So “will I enjoy the day-to-day work?” is not a trivial question. Since work occupies a large fraction of waking life, persistent boredom, lack of meaning, or poor fit can matter a lot.

## 2. “Interesting work” maps onto some of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

The job-design literature usually does not use the phrase “interesting work” exactly. It talks about things like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- complexity,
- opportunity to use abilities,
- meaningfulness.

These are basically the academic decomposition of “this work is interesting and satisfying.”

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, meta-analysis of work design, found that motivational job characteristics — especially **autonomy, task significance, feedback, task variety, and skill variety** — were strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower withdrawal.

Older but foundational:

- **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**
- **Fried & Ferris 1987** meta-analysis

The consistent result: jobs that let people use varied skills, exercise judgment, see meaningful impact, and get feedback tend to produce higher satisfaction.

For your case, “very interesting research engineering” likely scores high on at least some of these: skill use, complexity, learning, maybe task significance. A less interesting managerial role might score lower on intellectual task interest but higher on autonomy, influence, and people development. The net effect depends on which needs matter more to you.

## 3. Person–job fit and interest fit are robustly related to satisfaction

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, meta-analysis, found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. The corrected correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction is often reported around `ρ ≈ .5+`, which is large for organizational psychology.
- Vocational interest congruence studies, e.g. **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012**, find that when people’s interests fit their work, they tend to have higher satisfaction, better performance, and greater persistence, though the effects are usually more modest than broad person–job fit.

This is relevant because “less interesting” may really mean “worse fit with my curiosity, strengths, and preferred way of spending attention.” If so, the research would treat that as a serious cost.

## 4. Meaningful work and “calling” are strongly associated with well-being

There is also a literature on meaningful work.

- **Wrzesniewski et al. 1997** distinguished people who see work as a job, career, or calling. People with a calling orientation tend to report higher job and life satisfaction, though causality is complicated.
- **Allan et al. 2019**, meta-analysis, found meaningful work associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

If your current work feels not just “fun” but **meaningful**, e.g. important research, aligned with your values, high impact, then leaving that for a less meaningful role could have a real well-being cost.

But again, leadership can also be meaningful. For some people, helping a small team grow, setting direction, and multiplying others’ work is deeply meaningful.

## 5. Salary matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller than people expect

There are two related literatures: salary and job satisfaction, and income and general subjective well-being.

On salary and job satisfaction:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, meta-analysis, found that pay level is positively but modestly related to job satisfaction — roughly `r ≈ .15` with job satisfaction and somewhat higher with pay satisfaction.

So pay matters, but it is usually not among the strongest predictors of whether people like their jobs.

On income and happiness more broadly:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that evaluative life satisfaction kept rising with income, while emotional well-being appeared to plateau around a certain level, though this result has been debated.
- **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly log-linearly, with no sharp plateau.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being continues to rise with income, but for the least happy group, emotional well-being may plateau after a relatively high income.

The practical interpretation: more money is good, but the effect is roughly proportional to **percentage change**, not absolute dollars, and likely has diminishing marginal utility. If both offers are already financially comfortable and Meta is only “a bit higher,” research would not predict that salary alone will dominate the effect of day-to-day work quality.

## 6. Promotion and leadership can increase satisfaction, but also stress

A move to team leadership is not merely about salary/status. It changes the job’s psychological structure.

Leadership may increase:

- autonomy,
- status,
- influence,
- responsibility,
- relatedness,
- opportunity to mentor,
- career capital.

But it may also increase:

- meetings,
- conflict,
- ambiguity,
- accountability without control,
- reduced deep work,
- emotional labor,
- stress.

Research on promotions is mixed but useful.

- **Johnston & Lee 2013**, “Extra Status and Extra Stress,” found promotions can increase job satisfaction but may also bring stress and worse health-related outcomes.
- The **job demand–control model** from **Karasek 1979** and later job demands–resources research suggest that high-demand jobs can be energizing when paired with high control and resources, but harmful when demands are high and control/support are insufficient.

So the key question is: would this Meta role be a **high-control, high-learning leadership opportunity**, or a **high-meeting, low-intellectual-stimulation, execution-management role**? Those are very different psychologically.

## 7. Regret research: people often regret career inactions, but it does not answer your exact question

Regret research gives weaker but still relevant signals.

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that education and career are among the most common domains of major life regret.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that regrets of **inaction** often grow over time, while regrets of action are often sharper in the short term but fade more.

This cuts both ways.

If “leading a team” is something you have always wanted to try, then never trying management could become a long-term regret. But if you leave unusually interesting, meaningful research for a role you already suspect is less interesting, you may also regret giving up a rare fit.

The regret literature does not say “take the promotion” or “stay with interesting work.” It says people tend to regret not pursuing personally important possibilities, especially when they remain counterfactual and unexplored.

## My synthesis of the evidence

Based on the research, I would weight the factors roughly like this:

1. **Day-to-day intrinsic work quality matters a lot.** Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the most reliable predictors of job satisfaction.
2. **Person–job fit matters a lot.** If your current role is unusually well matched to your curiosity and values, that is valuable.
3. **Small salary differences matter, but usually less than intrinsic work quality**, especially at high income levels.
4. **Leadership opportunity can matter a lot if it is intrinsically motivating**, not merely higher status. Wanting to lead a team is a legitimate intrinsic preference.
5. **The dangerous case** is taking a less interesting role for pay/status while overestimating how much the title and compensation will compensate for reduced daily engagement.
6. **The promising case** is taking a leadership role where the work is less personally fascinating but still meaningful, high-autonomy, growthful, and aligned with your long-term identity.

A research-backed way to frame the decision is not:

> interesting work vs money

but rather:

> Which role gives me more autonomy, mastery, meaning, relatedness, growth, and long-term fit?

For your specific case, I would investigate the Meta role along these lines:

- How much real autonomy would you have?
- Would you shape technical direction or mostly coordinate execution?
- How much time would be management vs technical work?
- Is the team working on something you can become genuinely interested in?
- Would you get mentorship as a new manager?
- Is there a path back to high-quality research/IC work if management is not for you?
- Can you get a similar team-lead opportunity at Anthropic without giving up the interesting work?

If you can create a hybrid — interesting work plus small-team leadership — the research would suggest that is likely better than trading away intrinsic fit for salary alone. But if leading people is itself a deep, long-standing goal and the Meta role offers real autonomy and growth, then the move could be rational despite the work being less intellectually interesting.

answer:
I don’t know of a clean study that randomizes people into “more interesting lower-pay job” vs “less interesting higher-pay job” for years. But several adjacent literatures are quite relevant. The short version is:

**Yes: intrinsically interesting/meaningful work, autonomy, learning, and fit are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and are meaningfully related to overall life satisfaction. Pay matters, but at already-high incomes the marginal effect of “a bit higher salary” is usually much smaller. Regret evidence is more indirect: people often regret missed career/identity opportunities, not just missed income.**

## 1. “Interesting work” is usually studied as enriched job design

The closest research area is the **Job Characteristics Model**: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. These are basically “is the work engaging, meaningful, and self-directed?”

Key results:

- **Hackman & Oldham**, and later meta-analyses by **Loher et al. 1985**, **Fried & Ferris 1987**, and **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, find that jobs high in autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and complexity are substantially associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction,
  - higher intrinsic motivation,
  - stronger commitment,
  - lower turnover intentions,
  - sometimes better performance.

The effect sizes are not tiny. Across studies, these job-design variables often correlate with job satisfaction in the rough **r ≈ .3–.6** range, depending on construct and measurement. That is large by organizational psychology standards.

Especially relevant: the relationship tends to be stronger for people high in **growth need strength**—people who actively want learning, challenge, mastery, and autonomy. A research engineer doing frontier work likely fits that profile more than the average worker.

## 2. Job satisfaction spills into life satisfaction

This is not just “you like work more but life is unchanged.”

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** meta-analysis found a substantial correlation between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, often cited around **r ≈ .4**.
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang 2010** reviewed/meta-analyzed the job satisfaction–subjective well-being relationship and found meaningful associations, with evidence for bidirectional effects: happier people like jobs more, but better jobs also seem to improve well-being.
- **Faragher, Cass & Cooper 2005** found job satisfaction is strongly related to mental health outcomes, especially burnout, anxiety/depression, and general psychological distress.

So the evidence does suggest that people in more satisfying/interesting jobs tend to be happier overall, not just happier from 9–5.

## 3. Meaningful work is a particularly strong predictor

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research/AI-safety-type work they may be intertwined.

- A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. 2019** on meaningful work found strong associations between meaningful work and:
  - job satisfaction,
  - engagement,
  - organizational commitment,
  - life satisfaction,
  - lower turnover intentions,
  - lower burnout.

The job satisfaction correlation is often reported as quite high, roughly in the **r ≈ .5–.6** range depending on measures. Meaningful work is one of the more powerful work-related predictors of well-being.

Important caveat: meaningful work can also lead people to tolerate overwork. The **Job Demands–Resources** literature suggests that high challenge is good when paired with autonomy, support, recovery, and resources; high challenge plus chronic overload predicts burnout.

## 4. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** meta-analysis found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, lower intent to quit, and performance.
- Vocational-interest fit also predicts satisfaction and persistence, though narrower “interest congruence” measures tend to show more modest effects than broader fit/meaning/autonomy measures.

For your case, the question is not merely “Anthropic vs Meta” or “IC vs manager.” It is: **Which role better fits your actual motivational profile?**

If “leading a small team” is a deep personal aspiration, that can itself be a strong fit factor. But if the actual day-to-day work is mostly meetings, planning, stakeholder management, performance calibration, and less intellectual depth, then the fit may be worse despite the title.

## 5. Salary matters, but marginal effects are usually smaller at high incomes

Pay is real; the research does not say “money doesn’t matter.” But it usually matters less than people expect once basic security and comfort are met.

- **Judge et al. 2010** meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction found that actual pay level has a relatively weak relationship with job satisfaction, often summarized around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than pay level itself, suggesting fairness, comparison, and expectations are important.
- For overall well-being, **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found income strongly predicts life evaluation but emotional well-being showed diminishing returns at higher incomes. Later work by **Killingsworth 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** suggests experienced well-being can continue rising with income, but roughly with **log income**—i.e. proportional gains matter more than absolute gains.

So if Meta’s offer is “a bit higher” and you are already financially secure, the expected well-being gain from salary alone is probably modest. If the raise materially changes your housing, family security, runway, immigration situation, ability to donate, or financial stress, then it matters more.

## 6. Leadership could compensate if it increases autonomy, mastery, relatedness, and significance

The “leading a small team” part should not be dismissed. Research from **Self-Determination Theory**—Deci & Ryan and many later studies—suggests that well-being and motivation are supported by:

- autonomy,
- competence/mastery,
- relatedness,
- purpose.

Management can increase these: you may have more leverage, mentorship, ownership, and strategic influence. But management can also decrease them if it becomes bureaucracy, coordination, and responsibility without intellectual engagement.

So the empirical question is: **Does the Meta role give you more of the core job-design goods, or merely more title/pay?**

A less interesting technical domain can be offset if the leadership work itself is deeply meaningful to you.

## 7. Regret research is indirect but useful

There is not much direct evidence like “people who chose interesting work over money later regret/don’t regret it.” But regret studies show patterns.

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that career is one of the major domains of life regret, along with education and romance. People regret missed opportunities in domains where they had choice.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that short-term regrets are often about actions, while long-term regrets are more often about inactions—things not tried.
- **Davidai & Gilovich 2018** found that people’s most enduring regrets often concern failures to live up to their “ideal self,” not just their “ought self.”

Applied here, regret depends on which unrealized path feels more central to your ideal self:

- “I wanted to become a leader and never tried.”
- “I left unusually meaningful frontier research for a less interesting role because of salary/title.”
- “I missed a chance to build management skills.”
- “I gave up a rare intellectual environment I couldn’t easily get back.”

The research does not tell you which of those will dominate. It says long-term regret is often about **identity-relevant missed possibilities**.

## 8. People often overweight salient, easy-to-compare factors

Decision-psychology research is also relevant.

- **Hsee & Zhang** on “distinction bias”: when comparing options side by side, people overweight attributes that are easy to compare, even if those attributes matter less in lived experience.
- **Schkade & Kahneman 1998** on the focusing illusion: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”
- **Stutzer & Frey 2008** on commuting: people often accept longer commutes for better jobs/houses, but commuting has persistent negative effects on well-being; people underweight repeated daily experience.

Salary, title, and “leading a team” are very easy to compare. Daily intellectual interest, meeting load, manager quality, autonomy, and whether you feel proud of the work are harder to compare but may matter more once you are living the job.

## My evidence-based summary

If you reduce the offer to:

> **“A bit higher salary + small-team leadership” vs “much more interesting work”**

then the literature would generally put a lot of weight on the **interesting/meaningful/autonomous work** side, especially at high income levels.

But the leadership component is a serious counterweight if it satisfies a central personal goal. The key is to evaluate the Meta role not as “less interesting work plus management,” but as a new job design:

- How much autonomy would you have?
- How intellectually alive would the work feel?
- How much of your calendar would be mentoring/strategy vs process/meetings?
- Would you be proud of the mission/output?
- Would this build a version of yourself you strongly want to become?
- Is the salary difference life-changing or merely nice?
- How reversible is the move?

The research does not say “always choose interesting work.” It says: **day-to-day intrinsic motivation, meaning, autonomy, and fit are major predictors of happiness; pay and status are weaker predictors once you are already secure; regret tends to attach to identity-relevant missed opportunities.**

answer:
Yes. The relevant research is mostly from organizational psychology, vocational psychology, and subjective-well-being economics. The short version is:

> For people already in secure, well-paid jobs, day-to-day work content — autonomy, intellectual challenge, learning, meaning, fit with interests, and quality of colleagues/manager — tends to predict job satisfaction more strongly than marginal increases in pay.  
> But “leading a team” may itself be an intrinsic motivator if it gives you autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth. So the key question is not “interesting work vs salary/title,” but whether the new role improves or worsens the psychologically important parts of the job.

Below are some research findings that seem most relevant.

---

## 1. Interesting work / job design is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied through constructs like:

- autonomy / control over work  
- skill variety  
- task complexity  
- learning and growth  
- task significance / meaningfulness  
- feedback  
- person–job fit  
- intrinsic motivation

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. Later meta-analyses generally support it.

A major meta-analysis:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
They reviewed work-design research and found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — are consistently associated with:

- higher job satisfaction  
- higher intrinsic motivation  
- better performance  
- lower stress/burnout  
- lower turnover intentions

Correlations vary by feature and study, but are often in the moderate range, roughly around `r = .30–.50` for job satisfaction. In social-science terms, that is substantial.

This is directly relevant to research-engineering work: cognitively demanding, creative jobs tend to benefit especially from autonomy, problem-solving, and intrinsic motivation.

---

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit matter a lot

Another large literature studies **person–environment fit**: whether the job matches your abilities, values, interests, and preferred style of work.

A widely cited meta-analysis:

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, Personnel Psychology**  
They found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  
- lower intent to quit  
- performance, though more modestly

The corrected correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction was around `r ≈ .50+`, which is large for this kind of research.

Vocational-interest fit — e.g. whether the work matches your intellectual/technical interests — also predicts satisfaction and persistence, though effects are usually more modest than broad person–job fit.

Takeaway: if Anthropic gives you unusually high fit with your curiosity, values, and preferred problems, that is not a trivial factor. It is one of the better-supported predictors of work satisfaction.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases usually matter less than people expect — especially at high income

Pay does predict satisfaction, but the relationship is usually weaker than people assume.

A useful meta-analysis:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, Journal of Vocational Behavior**  
They found that **pay level** has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction — often around `r = .15–.25`, depending on measure. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but still usually less than job content, autonomy, fit, and relationships.

On broader life satisfaction:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled these: for most people, well-being keeps rising with income, but the slope is smaller at high incomes; for the least happy subgroup, it plateaus more.

So: money matters, but if you are already in a high-paying AI research role and Meta is “a bit higher,” the expected happiness gain from salary alone is probably modest unless it meaningfully changes your security, freedom, family plans, or optionality.

---

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in research roles. There is a sizable literature on meaningful work.

A meta-analysis:

**Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019, Journal of Management Studies**  
Meaningful work was strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- life satisfaction  
- lower depression and withdrawal intentions

Effects are often moderate-to-large. Meaningful work is one of the stronger non-pay predictors of work well-being.

For someone at Anthropic, the “meaning” dimension may be unusually salient if you care about AI safety, frontier-model research, or Anthropic’s mission. If Meta’s role is less interesting and less meaningful, research would treat that as a real cost, not merely a preference.

---

## 5. Leadership/promotion has mixed effects: more autonomy and status, but also more stress

The Meta offer has another important factor: leading a small team. That is not merely an extrinsic perk. It could provide:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- mentoring/teaching  
- status  
- broader impact  
- career capital  
- a new source of intrinsic motivation

Research on promotion and management roles is mixed. Promotions often increase job satisfaction, at least initially, but can also increase stress and reduce health.

For example, studies using panel data, such as **Johnston & Lee, 2013, “Extra status and extra stress”**, suggest that promotions can bring higher satisfaction/status/pay but also higher strain.

So the research does not say “management is better” or “management is worse.” It says you should examine the concrete work design:

- Will you have real decision authority?  
- Will you still do technically interesting work?  
- Will you mostly coordinate, report upward, and handle process?  
- Will the team’s mission be meaningful to you?  
- Is the manager above you excellent?  
- Is the org healthy?  
- Is the leadership role a real growth path or just a heavier workload?

A leadership role can be intrinsically satisfying if it satisfies autonomy, competence, relatedness, and impact. It can be draining if it mostly adds bureaucracy and accountability without meaningful control.

---

## 6. Job changes often have a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern

There is evidence that people often feel happier shortly after changing jobs, but some of that effect fades.

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005, Journal of Applied Psychology** studied the “honeymoon-hangover” effect: job satisfaction often rises after a move, then declines as novelty wears off and frustrations become salient.

This is relevant because salary, title, and novelty may be especially salient before switching. The more durable predictors are likely the recurring daily/weekly experiences: problems you work on, autonomy, colleagues, manager, pace, and meaning.

---

## 7. Regret research is indirect but relevant

There is not much direct research of the form: “People who chose more interesting work over higher pay later regretted or did not regret it.” But general regret research offers clues.

**Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that people’s biggest life regrets often involve education and career. Career is one of the top regret domains.

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that over the long run, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions — the things they did not try.

**Davidai & Gilovich, 2018** found that people’s most enduring regrets are often about failing to live up to their “ideal self” rather than their “ought self.” That is, people regret not pursuing personally meaningful aspirations more than not meeting external expectations.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- If “doing frontier, fascinating AI research at Anthropic” is central to your ideal self, leaving for less interesting work could become a regret.
- If “leading and developing a team” is a long-standing aspiration, not trying management could also become a regret.

So regret research does not answer the object-level choice, but it suggests you should identify which option better serves your own “ideal self,” not just which has more external validation.

---

## Practical research-based summary

The evidence would suggest weighting the factors roughly like this:

1. **Daily work content and intrinsic interest** — very important.  
2. **Autonomy and control** — very important.  
3. **Meaning / mission / values fit** — very important.  
4. **Quality of manager, team, and culture** — very important.  
5. **Growth and skill development** — important, especially early/mid-career.  
6. **Pay** — important, but marginal increases matter less once already high.  
7. **Title/status** — can matter, but people may adapt to it quickly.  
8. **Leadership opportunity** — potentially very important if it is intrinsically motivating, not merely a title.

So the most research-consistent conclusion is:

> If the Meta role is only modestly better on pay/status but substantially worse on intrinsic interest, meaning, and day-to-day fit, the evidence would generally favor staying with the more interesting role.  
> But if the leadership component is itself deeply motivating and gives you autonomy, impact, and growth, then it may compensate for — or even outweigh — the lower technical interest.

A useful framing would be: do not compare “Anthropic interesting work” vs “Meta higher salary/team lead.” Compare the actual predicted weekly life in each role across autonomy, learning, meaning, people, stress, and growth. Those are the variables the research says are most likely to matter long-term.

answer:
Short version from the research: **yes, “interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work” is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.** Pay matters, but once pay is already high enough to remove financial stress, **additional salary tends to have a smaller marginal association with happiness than day-to-day job content, autonomy, fit, manager/team quality, and work demands.** Direct evidence on “regret after choosing the more interesting job over higher pay” is much thinner.

## 1. Interesting work / job fit strongly predicts job satisfaction

A large literature in organizational psychology studies this under labels like **job characteristics**, **person–job fit**, **intrinsic motivation**, and **meaningful work**.

### Job characteristics model

Classic and meta-analytic work on the Job Characteristics Model finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- task identity,
- feedback,
- complexity / problem-solving,
- opportunity to use valued skills.

Meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris, 1987** and **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** find that these “motivational work characteristics” correlate substantially with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal/turnover intentions. Many of the corrected correlations are in the rough range of **r ≈ .30–.50**, which is large by field standards.

For your case, “doing very interesting research” maps closely to job complexity, skill use, problem-solving, autonomy, and task significance.

### Person–job fit

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that **person–job fit** is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction. The corrected correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction was roughly **ρ ≈ .56**, and it was also strongly negatively related to intention to quit.

So if Anthropic is a much better fit for your abilities, interests, values, and desired daily work, the literature would predict meaningfully higher job satisfaction.

Vocational-interest fit specifically tends to show more modest but still positive effects. For example, meta-analytic work on Holland-type interest congruence generally finds positive relationships with satisfaction and performance, often around **r ≈ .15–.25**. Broader person–job fit is stronger than narrow interest congruence.

## 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but often overlap in research jobs.

Meta-analyses on **meaningful work** find strong associations with:

- job satisfaction,
- engagement,
- organizational commitment,
- lower turnover intention,
- lower burnout,
- somewhat higher life satisfaction.

For example, work by **Allan et al.** on meaningful work finds correlations with job satisfaction and engagement that are often quite high, while correlations with broader life satisfaction are smaller but still meaningful.

This is consistent with **self-determination theory**: people tend to do better when work satisfies needs for:

- **autonomy** — I have agency,
- **competence** — I am growing and using my abilities,
- **relatedness** — I am connected to people I respect,
- sometimes also **purpose / contribution**.

Meta-analytic work on basic psychological need satisfaction at work, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**, finds that need satisfaction is robustly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout.

## 3. Pay matters, but marginal pay matters less than people expect — especially at high income

The pay literature is more mixed, but the broad finding is: **pay matters, but the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly modest.**

A useful meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, which found that pay level correlated only weakly with:

- overall job satisfaction: roughly **r ≈ .15**,
- pay satisfaction: roughly **r ≈ .20–.25**.

This does not mean money is unimportant. It means that, across people, salary explains much less of job satisfaction than things like job fit, autonomy, meaningfulness, manager quality, and work design.

On income and general happiness:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that evaluative life satisfaction keeps rising with income, while experienced emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75k/year in their U.S. data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the plateau result using experience sampling and found experienced well-being continued rising with log income.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled the two: for a less-happy minority, emotional well-being plateaus around moderate-high income, while for others it continues increasing, but with diminishing returns.

The important practical point is that the relationship is roughly **logarithmic**: each doubling of income matters more similarly than each extra dollar. So at already-high tech/research salaries, a “bit higher salary” may have a small effect unless it meaningfully changes your life constraints: housing, family plans, financial independence timeline, stress, visa/security concerns, etc.

## 4. Leading a small team could matter a lot — but depends on what need it satisfies

Your Meta offer has an important non-salary factor: **leading a small team**, which you say you always wanted to do.

The research would not treat this as “extrinsic” in the same way as salary. Management/leadership can satisfy intrinsic needs:

- autonomy,
- influence,
- mentoring,
- status,
- impact,
- learning,
- identity growth.

Those can be very meaningful.

But management also often adds:

- meetings,
- emotional labor,
- coordination burden,
- accountability without full control,
- less deep technical/research time,
- possible political work.

The **job demands–resources** literature is relevant here. High demands are not necessarily bad if paired with high resources and autonomy; “challenge demands” can increase engagement, while “hindrance demands” increase strain. See e.g. **Bakker & Demerouti** on JD-R theory and **Crawford, LePine & Rich, 2010** on challenge vs hindrance stressors.

So the key research-based distinction is:

> A leadership role is likely satisfying if it increases autonomy, competence, impact, and relatedness.  
> It is less likely satisfying if it mainly increases coordination burden while reducing the intrinsically interesting work you value.

## 5. Regret research: career regrets are common, but direct evidence is limited

There is less direct research on “people who chose interesting work over money later regretted it.”

But general regret research finds:

- **Career and education are among the most common life-regret domains.** See **Roese & Summerville, 2005**.
- Long-term regrets are often regrets of **inaction** rather than action. See **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**.
- People especially regret failures to pursue their “ideal self” — the person they wished to become — more than failures of duty/obligation. See **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**.

This does not directly answer your job choice. But it suggests that regret is often about missed identity-defining paths: “I never tried to lead,” “I left the field I found most meaningful,” “I optimized for money and drifted,” etc.

In your case, both options have possible regret stories:

- Anthropic regret story: “I never took the chance to lead a team when I had it.”
- Meta regret story: “I left unusually interesting research for a less meaningful role and became less intellectually alive.”

The literature does not tell you which regret is more likely; it says people regret deviations from deeply held goals and identities.

## 6. What the research most strongly implies

Based on the evidence, I would summarize it this way:

1. **Day-to-day work content matters a lot.** Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is robustly linked to job satisfaction and engagement.

2. **Fit matters more than generic prestige or pay.** Person–job fit has one of the strongest relationships with satisfaction.

3. **Salary matters, but marginal salary matters less at high income.** A modest pay bump is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in intrinsic interest unless the money materially changes your life.

4. **Leadership is a real intrinsic factor, not merely a career trophy.** If leading a team is a core developmental goal, it deserves serious weight.

5. **The best move may be to negotiate role design, not just choose company.** The research suggests optimizing for the bundle: interesting work + autonomy + growth + good people + sustainable demands. So the useful question is whether you can get:
   - more team leadership at Anthropic, or
   - more interesting/ambitious research content at Meta.

If you want a research-grounded decision rule, it would be:

> Prefer the option with the better expected combination of person–job fit, autonomy, meaningfulness, competence growth, team quality, and sustainable demands — unless the salary difference materially changes your financial security or long-term freedom.

In most studies, those intrinsic/work-design factors predict satisfaction more strongly than pay level does.

answer:
There isn’t a clean RCT of “interesting job vs. slightly higher-paid less-interesting job,” but there is a large adjacent literature in organizational psychology, labor economics, and subjective well-being. The broad result is:

> **After basic financial comfort and perceived pay fairness, the content of the work — autonomy, intellectual challenge, meaning, skill use, fit with interests, and quality of colleagues/manager — tends to predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than absolute pay.**  
> But leadership can count as “interesting work” if the day-to-day activity of leading, mentoring, setting direction, and having impact is intrinsically motivating to you.

Below are the most relevant research strands.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” maps onto some of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In the job-design literature, “interesting work” is usually decomposed into things like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**
- **feedback**
- **complexity/problem-solving**
- **learning/mastery**
- **person–job fit**

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. Later meta-analyses generally support it.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** found that motivational job characteristics — especially autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and complexity — are consistently associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. Correlations are often in the moderate range, roughly around **r = .3–.5**, depending on the characteristic and outcome.

Similarly, **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, a meta-analysis on person–environment fit, found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction and organizational commitment and negatively related to intention to quit. Person–job fit tends to be one of the larger predictors in this area.

So the literature does not treat “interesting work” as a fluffy luxury. It is close to the core of what predicts job satisfaction.

---

## 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research roles they often overlap. The meaningful-work literature finds strong associations with work engagement, job satisfaction, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

For example, **Allan et al. 2019** reviewed/meta-analyzed meaningful work and found that meaningful work is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- engagement
- life meaning
- organizational commitment
- lower turnover intentions

The correlations with job satisfaction and engagement are often large by social-science standards.

This is relevant if your Anthropic role feels meaningful because the research questions matter to you, while the Meta role feels less intrinsically meaningful.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but absolute pay is usually a weaker predictor than people expect — especially at high income

The best-known meta-analysis here is **Judge et al. 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They found that **actual pay level** is only weakly associated with job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but pay satisfaction includes fairness, comparison, expectations, and feeling valued.

In subjective well-being research, income reliably predicts happiness, but with diminishing marginal returns: the effect is closer to **log income** than linear income. So going from financial stress to comfort matters a lot; going from already-high compensation to slightly higher compensation usually matters less.

Relevant studies:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income but appeared to flatten around a threshold in their data.
- **Killingsworth 2021**: found well-being continues rising with income, but roughly logarithmically.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023**: reconciled some of the earlier disagreement; for many people well-being continues rising with income, but the effect is not huge and varies by baseline happiness.

For someone already in a very well-compensated research-engineering role, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely, by itself, to dominate differences in daily work quality, autonomy, meaning, and intellectual engagement — unless that pay difference materially affects your financial security, family plans, immigration situation, housing, etc.

---

## 4. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are meaningfully linked

One reason work content matters is that job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction are not separate boxes. Meta-analyses find a moderate relationship between them.

For example:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** found a substantial relationship between job and life satisfaction.
- **Bowling et al. 2010** reviewed evidence suggesting the relationship is reciprocal: people who are happier tend to be more satisfied at work, but satisfying work also contributes to life satisfaction.

So “this job is less interesting, but life outside work will compensate” can be true for some people, but the evidence suggests work quality often spills over into general well-being.

---

## 5. Leadership/promotion is mixed: it can increase satisfaction, but only if it improves autonomy, impact, and fit

The Meta offer has two different components:

1. **higher salary**
2. **leading a small team**

The second may be much more important than the first. Leadership can satisfy needs for:

- autonomy
- competence
- status
- impact
- mentoring/relatedness
- career growth
- identity development

Research on promotions generally finds that promotions can increase job satisfaction, but the effects can be complicated and sometimes temporary. Promotions also often bring more hours, more stress, more politics, and less time doing the technical work that originally motivated the person.

A useful distinction comes from the job-demands literature:

- **Challenge demands** — hard but meaningful responsibilities, stretch goals, complex coordination — can increase engagement.
- **Hindrance demands** — bureaucracy, politics, pointless meetings, ambiguity, blocked execution — tend to predict strain and burnout.

See e.g. **Crawford, LePine & Rich 2010** on challenge vs. hindrance demands.

So “leading a small team” is not uniformly good or bad. It depends on whether the actual weekly work looks like:

- setting technical direction,
- mentoring strong people,
- shaping research/product strategy,
- having real decision authority,

or instead:

- performance management,
- hiring loops,
- roadmap churn,
- cross-org politics,
- status meetings,
- firefighting.

The research would tell you to evaluate the **day-to-day leadership work**, not the title.

---

## 6. People often overweight concrete, comparable factors like salary/title

There is also decision-psychology research suggesting that when comparing options side by side, people overweight attributes that are easy to compare — salary, title, level, prestige — and underweight attributes that dominate daily experience.

Relevant concepts:

- **Focusing illusion** — Kahneman and colleagues: when you focus on a difference, you exaggerate its effect on future well-being.
- **Distinction bias** — Hsee & Zhang: in joint evaluation, people overemphasize differences that are salient in comparison but less important in lived experience.

In career decisions, salary and title are highly legible. “How much will I enjoy Tuesday afternoon six months from now?” is less legible but often more predictive of experienced happiness.

This does not mean salary/title are unimportant. It means they are prone to being over-weighted in comparison mode.

---

## 7. Regret evidence is weaker, but career/education regrets are common

Direct evidence on “people who chose interesting work over higher pay later regret it” is limited. The regret literature does not give a clean answer.

But some relevant findings:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that education and career are among the most common domains of regret.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** argued that in the short run people often regret actions, but in the long run they more often regret inactions — things not tried.
- “Calling” research suggests that people who feel they are living out a calling tend to report higher work and life satisfaction, but people with an unanswered calling can feel dissatisfaction or regret.

Applied to your situation, the regret question may not be “interesting work vs. salary.” It may be between two possible identities:

- “I stayed close to unusually interesting frontier research.”
- “I finally tried leading a team, something I had wanted for a long time.”

The research does not say which identity you personally will regret not pursuing. But it does suggest that unpursued intrinsic aspirations can remain psychologically salient.

---

## My research-based summary

If forced to compress the literature into a few decision-relevant claims:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**  
   It is not a minor perk.

2. **Absolute pay has a surprisingly weak relationship with job satisfaction once you are already financially comfortable.**  
   Pay fairness and financial security matter more than raw salary.

3. **Leadership can be a major positive if it gives autonomy, impact, mentorship, and growth.**  
   But it can be negative if it mostly replaces interesting technical work with coordination, politics, and bureaucracy.

4. **People often overweight salary/title because they are easy to compare.**  
   Daily task content, manager quality, autonomy, and colleagues are less visible but often more important.

5. **Direct regret evidence is limited.**  
   But long-run regret often concerns untried paths and unrealized identities, so your long-standing desire to lead should be taken seriously as an intrinsic factor, not merely an extrinsic career move.

A practical research-grounded test would be: ignore salary and title for a moment and ask, for each job, **“What will I actually do for 20–30 hours of a normal week, and how much of that work will feel absorbing, meaningful, and growth-producing?”** That variable is very strongly supported by the literature.
